Creating all 26 issues now, with priority mapped High/Medium/Low from the report's P1/P2/P3 and low-end estimates (S→1, M→2, the two genuine projects→3). Starting with the 7 P1 issues.

input.title: Remove redundant pnpm install settings and decide on the node-linker strategy
input.description:
**Why:** The workspace runs pnpm in fully non-strict mode (`hoisted` node-linker + shamefully-hoist), which removes pnpm's guarantee that a package can only import what it declares. This already permits phantom dependencies (see the js-core ticket) and makes upgrades riskier. Some settings are also no-ops (`legacyPeerDeps` is an npm option) or redundant once the hoisted linker is set.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- No-op / redundant pnpm settings removed from `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, or documented if actually load-bearing.
- A documented decision on the node-linker strategy: keep `hoisted` with a comment naming the tooling that requires it, or migrate to the isolated linker with all newly-surfaced undeclared dependencies fixed.
- Install, build and tests green after the change.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Activate the pnpm supply-chain release cooldown
input.description:
**Why:** The workspace declares a cooldown exclusion (`minimumReleaseAgeExclude`) but no `minimumReleaseAge` is actually set, so freshly published (possibly compromised) package versions install immediately. The orphaned exclusion also gives a false impression that a cooldown is already active — undermining the otherwise strong security-overrides posture.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- An agreed `minimumReleaseAge` cooldown set in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` (or the orphaned exclusion removed if a cooldown isn't wanted).
- `pnpm config` confirms the cooldown is in effect.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Unify the Node.js version across dev, CI and production
input.description:
**Why:** Development (`.nvmrc` = 22.12.0), CI (floating `22.x`) and the production Docker image (Node 24) currently run three different Node versions. Tests never run on the major version that serves production traffic, so Node-major-specific bugs can't be caught in CI.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- A single source of truth for the Node version; CI reads it rather than pinning a floating major.
- Dev and production agree on a major version, or the difference is explicitly documented and justified.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Migrate ESLint to v9 flat config
input.description:
**Why:** ESLint 8 is end-of-life (no security or bug fixes) and increasingly blocks upgrades of plugins that are dropping eslintrc/ESLint-8 support. The shared config also carries duplicated legacy and modern variants that drift independently — and the web app is currently linted by the older rule set.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- ESLint upgraded to v9+ with a single flat config exported from the shared config package.
- Legacy config variants removed; all apps and packages lint against one rule set.
- `pnpm lint` green across the workspace.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Stop next/image from allowing arbitrary remote hosts
input.description:
**Why:** `next.config` builds a curated `remotePatterns` allowlist and then unconditionally appends a wildcard that allows any HTTPS host, turning the image optimizer into an open proxy (bandwidth/CPU abuse, SSRF-adjacent surface, optimizer-cache poisoning). Combined with `dangerouslyAllowSVG`, the posture is more permissive than it looks.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `next/image` only optimizes images from an explicit allowlist; the blanket wildcard is removed or gated behind explicit config for self-hosters.
- Cloud image rendering still works; `dangerouslyAllowSVG` re-reviewed.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Add pnpm-store and Turbo remote caching to all CI workflows
input.description:
**Why:** No workflow caches the pnpm store, so every job runs a cold install, and the Turbo remote-cache token is set only on build/e2e/integration jobs — not on the highest-frequency lint, test and sonar jobs. This is likely the cheapest large CI-minutes win available.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- pnpm store caching enabled in the CI setup step across workflows.
- Turbo remote cache available to the lint, test and sonar jobs.
- CI wall-clock measurably reduced with no change in results.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Remove dead Vitest config and adopt the projects layout
input.description:
**Why:** `environmentMatchGlobs` was removed in Vitest 4 but is still configured in the web app and the surveys package, so jsdom/happy-dom assignment silently does nothing — component tests only pass because each file carries an `@vitest-environment` pragma. The root `vitest.workspace.ts` is also the deprecated approach.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Dead `environmentMatchGlobs` config removed.
- Test environments selected via the supported `projects` config; a new component test runs in the browser-like env without a per-file pragma.
- Root vitest workspace migrated to the `projects` option.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

content.text: All 7 P1 issues created (ENG-1674–1680). Now the 12 P2 (Medium) issues — first batch of 6.

input.title: Declare js-core's cross-package dependencies
input.description:
**Why:** The js-core package imports from other workspace packages (and reaches into the web app for a version value) without declaring any of them, which only works because of the hoisted node_modules layout. Turbo can't order builds or invalidate caches correctly for these hidden edges — a change in a consumed package won't rebuild js-core.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Every workspace package js-core consumes is declared as a proper workspace dependency.
- The reach-in to the web app's package.json is replaced with a build-time value owned by js-core.
- Turbo recognises the dependency edges.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Tighten turbo.json task hashing and env scoping
input.description:
**Why:** No task declares `inputs`, the shared `build` task carries one ~180-variable env allowlist (so changing any single variable busts every package's build cache), and unit tests are uncached — so cache hit-rates are far lower than they could be, compounding the CI-caching ticket.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Web-only env vars scoped to the web build task rather than the shared `build` task.
- Build inputs exclude test files; appropriate `globalDependencies` declared.
- A deliberate, documented decision on whether test tasks should be cached.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Resolve the duplicate migration directories in the database package
input.description:
**Why:** The database package has two near-identically-named migration directories — one live custom runner and one stale — and the Prisma config points at the gitignored one. Anyone debugging a migration can easily read or edit the wrong history.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- One canonical migration location; the stale directory removed or archived.
- The Prisma config and the custom runner agree on the path.
- The migration setup documented in the package README.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Delete orphaned packages/ui and the empty vendor stub
input.description:
**Why:** `packages/ui` has no package.json (only stale build output) so it isn't even a workspace member, and `vendor/minimatch-3.1.5` is an empty stub. Both mislead humans and tooling that grep into them, and an unexplained vendor entry reads as a supply-chain red flag during security review.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `packages/ui` and the empty vendor stub removed.
- `vendor/` carries a short note explaining the one legitimately vendored tarball.
- Install and build still green.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Route environment access through the validated env module
input.description:
**Why:** Despite a thorough validated env schema in the web app, ~140 direct `process.env` reads remain, so missing or mistyped variables fail at use-time (possibly in production) instead of at boot, and two competing conventions exist for contributors to copy.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- A lint rule flags direct `process.env` use outside the allowed bootstrap/config files.
- Existing direct reads migrated to the validated env object.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Move Sentry client init to instrumentation-client
input.description:
**Why:** Client-side Sentry is initialized inside a React component's `useEffect`, which runs after hydration and therefore misses module-evaluation, hydration and early-navigation errors — typically the most interesting client errors. The supported Next.js hook runs before app code.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Client Sentry initializes via the `instrumentation-client` entry point.
- Existing gating (DSN / production) and replay sample rates preserved.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

content.text: Second P2 batch (issues 14–19).

input.title: Fix the email package build and Tailwind version
input.description:
**Why:** The email package's entry points target raw source, its "build" only typechecks (a no-op that other tasks still wait on in the turbo graph), it has no tests, and it pins Tailwind v3 while the rest of the repo is on v4 — so v4-only classes would silently not render in emails.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- The package either produces real build output or is consistently treated as a source-consumed package and documented as such.
- Tailwind version reconciled with the rest of the repo, or the v3 pin documented as required by react-email.
- Basic test coverage added.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Normalize package entry points and version pinning
input.description:
**Why:** Packages disagree on their types paths (`dist/index.d.ts` vs `dist/src/index.d.ts`), two packages lack `exports` maps entirely, and one uses caret ranges despite the repo's exact-pin policy — inconsistencies that hurt DX and quietly escape the pinning convention.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Consistent types/exports layout across packages; `exports` maps added where missing.
- Caret ranges replaced with exact pins in the outlier package.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Adopt pnpm catalogs for shared dependency versions
input.description:
**Why:** There's no mechanism keeping shared toolchain versions aligned across packages; drift already exists (two different Vite majors, caret-vs-exact splits). Consistency currently relies on manual discipline and is masked by hoisting until it breaks.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Shared toolchain deps (typescript, vite, vitest, zod, react, @types/*) defined once via a pnpm catalog and referenced by packages.
- The Vite major-version split resolved in the same change.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Split lint check from autofix and add ESLint to pre-commit
input.description:
**Why:** lint-staged runs Prettier only (no ESLint pre-commit), and the `lint` scripts run with `--fix`, so CI "linting" silently rewrites files in the runner instead of failing on issues. One package also lints with the wrong (Next.js) shared config.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `lint` scripts check only; a separate fix script exists for local use.
- ESLint runs on staged files in the pre-commit hook.
- The mis-configured package uses the library lint config.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Harden the Content-Security-Policy
input.description:
**Why:** The global CSP allows `'unsafe-inline'` scripts and any HTTPS origin, which effectively neutralizes CSP as an XSS defense: any injected inline script or script from any HTTPS host executes. This is nontrivial — it needs a report-only rollout and testing across survey embeds and the renderer.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `script-src` no longer allows `'unsafe-inline'` or a blanket `https:` source; moved to a nonce / strict-dynamic approach with an explicit third-party allowlist.
- Rolled out report-only first, then enforced, with survey embeds and the renderer verified.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Document and verify Tailwind v4 source coverage for workspace packages
input.description:
**Why:** Tailwind v4 doesn't scan node_modules, so app styling relies on the unwritten invariant that consumed workspace UI packages ship their own compiled CSS. Storybook also mixes a v3-style config file with the v4 engine, which is confusing and fragile.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- The "workspace UI packages ship their own CSS / the app does not scan them" invariant made explicit (documented or via `@source`).
- Storybook moved to a single v4 CSS-first Tailwind config.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

content.text: Now the final 7 P3 (Low) issues, 20–26.

input.title: Remove runtime dependencies from the root package.json
input.description:
**Why:** `react` and `react-dom` sit in the root `dependencies`; a workspace root should carry only tooling. They're there to force hoisted resolution — an artifact of the non-strict node-linker.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `react`/`react-dom` removed from the root once resolution allows (packages that need them already declare them).

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Fix the lint:report output filename collision
input.description:
**Why:** Three packages all write their lint report to the same wrongly-named file, so the reports overwrite each other (a copy-paste artifact).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Each package writes a uniquely named lint report derived from the package name.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Align the per-package script matrix
input.description:
**Why:** Some packages are missing `typecheck` or `test:coverage` scripts, so turbo silently skips them and their coverage is invisible to the coverage/Sonar pipeline.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Every package exposes a consistent build/lint/typecheck/test script set, with any genuine exceptions documented.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Clean up shared tsconfig oddities
input.description:
**Why:** The base tsconfig uses legacy node module resolution, some display labels are inaccurate, and the Storybook app doesn't extend the shared config — small traps for anyone adding a new package.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Base module resolution modernized (or its use documented), display labels corrected.
- Storybook extends the shared tsconfig where practical.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Fix config-package naming and the stale README
input.description:
**Why:** The config packages mix two naming conventions, and one still ships the Turborepo starter README placeholder.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Config-package names follow one consistent convention.
- The placeholder README replaced with accurate content.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Remove or wire up dead root config
input.description:
**Why:** The root package.json carries a bundle-analysis budget block that no workflow uses, and the root eslintrc exists mainly to patch a single package.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Bundle-analysis either re-wired into CI or removed.
- The root eslint override folded into the flat-config migration.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

input.title: Load typegen env from a file instead of inline placeholders
input.description:
**Why:** The web `typegen` script embeds literal placeholder secret values inline; harmless, but they read like leaked credentials in a grep and drift from the env example.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `typegen` loads its environment from a file (like the test script does) rather than inline values.

_From the monorepo configuration audit._

content.text:
All 26 issues are created in the **Engineering** team, each assigned to you, labeled **refactor**, with priority mapped from the report (P1→High, P2→Medium, P3→Low) and low-end estimates (your team's scale renders them as XS/S, with the two genuine projects — ESLint flat config and CSP — at M).

They all landed in **Backlog** status (the team default). I followed your Linear-ticket convention — each is high-level with a **Why** and outcome-focused **Acceptance criteria**, no line numbers or step-by-step prescriptions, and a "From the monorepo configuration audit" footer so the source is traceable. Two small notes: I kept minimal config-file names in the bodies where the file *is* the subject (e.g. `pnpm-workspace.yaml`, `turbo.json`) since they're genuinely needed to locate the work; and I cross-referenced the js-core ticket from the pnpm-linker one since they're related. Let me know if you'd like them moved to a project/cycle or a different status.

Now with the issues created, please first solve the high priority ones. Make sure to use the Linear rules properly so we have a new branch + PR for every issue linked to the linear issues.

solve them one by one and send me the PR so I can have a look. Validate all PRs with pnpm lint, pnpm test and pnpm i.

After you opened all PRs, I'll review them and we'll make sure that its a all good before requesting external audits from my team-mates.

input.command:
gh pr create --title "chore(pnpm): activate supply-chain release cooldown" --body "## Problem

\`pnpm-workspace.yaml\` declares a \`minimumReleaseAgeExclude\` for \`@formbricks/hub\`, but no \`minimumReleaseAge\` is set anywhere (\`pnpm config get minimumReleaseAge\` → \`undefined\`). The cooldown the exclusion implies was never active: freshly published — potentially compromised — package versions could be resolved immediately, and the exclusion gave a false impression of protection.

## Solution

Set \`minimumReleaseAge: 4320\` (3 days, in minutes) in \`pnpm-workspace.yaml\`, with a comment explaining the policy. Most npm supply-chain compromises are detected and yanked within 1–2 days, so a 3-day cooldown blocks the dangerous window while keeping normal dependency updates flowing. The existing \`@formbricks/hub\` exclusion (first-party SDK published by our own CI) is unchanged. Only **new** resolutions are gated — the current lockfile and \`--frozen-lockfile\` CI installs are unaffected.

## Validation

- \`pnpm install\` ✅ \`Done in 11.4s using pnpm v11.7.0\`
- \`pnpm lint\` ✅ all tasks successful
- \`pnpm test\` ✅ \`Tasks: 21 successful, 21 total — Time: 42.242s\`

Fixes ENG-1675

input.command:
gh pr create --title "chore(pnpm): remove no-op install settings, document node-linker decision" --body "## Problem

\`pnpm-workspace.yaml\` carried two settings that do nothing:

- \`legacyPeerDeps: true\` — an **npm** option that pnpm doesn't recognize (not in pnpm's settings docs); a silent no-op carried over from an npm-era config.
- \`shamefullyHoist: true\` — only consulted by the **isolated** linker; with \`nodeLinker: hoisted\` the tree is already fully flat, so this is redundant.

Dead settings suggest config accretion and make the install behavior harder to reason about. The bigger question — why \`nodeLinker: hoisted\` at all — was undocumented.

## Solution

- Removed both no-op settings.
- Documented the \`nodeLinker: hoisted\` decision inline: it stays for now because packages still rely on hoisted undeclared deps (ENG-1681: js-core cross-package imports, ENG-1693: root react/react-dom); migrating to the strict \`isolated\` linker is the long-term goal tracked on ENG-1674, after those are fixed.

**Layout verified unchanged:** after a full \`node_modules\` purge + reinstall with the new settings, the tree is still flat (1269 hoisted top-level packages; \`react\`/\`jsdom\`/\`lodash\` still at the root), and \`pnpm-lock.yaml\` has zero diff — so nothing that depends on hoisting can be affected.

## Validation

- \`pnpm install\` (full purge + reinstall) ✅ \`Done in 30.1s using pnpm v11.7.0\`
- \`pnpm lint\` ✅ all tasks successful
- \`pnpm test\` ✅ \`Tasks: 21 successful, 21 total — Time: 46.214s\`

Part of ENG-1674 (the isolated-linker migration remains open on the issue)

input.command:
gh pr create --title "chore(node): unify Node.js version across dev, CI and production" --body "## Problem

The Node version was expressed in four places with three different values:

- \`.nvmrc\`: \`22.12.0\` (exact)
- CI: \`node-version: 22.x\` (floating latest 22) in 9 workflows + 1 composite action
- Production Dockerfile: \`node:24-alpine3.23\` — a **different major**
- \`engines\`: wide multi-range

Dev, CI and production all ran different Node versions; unit and e2e tests never executed on the major that serves production traffic, so Node-24-specific behavior differences (V8, undici/fetch, OpenSSL) were structurally invisible to CI.

## Solution

\`.nvmrc\` becomes the single source of truth, aligned to the **production major (Node 24)**:

- \`.nvmrc\` → \`24.14.0\` (production already runs \`node:24-alpine\`; local dev machines are on 24.14.0)
- Every workflow and the \`cache-build-web\` composite action now use \`node-version-file: \".nvmrc\"\` instead of hardcoded versions — bumping Node is now a one-file change
- Dockerfile documents that its base-image major must track \`.nvmrc\`
- \`engines\` stays wide deliberately (self-hosters on 20/22/24); the supported/tested version is what \`.nvmrc\` says

## Validation

- \`pnpm install\` ✅ \`Done in 26.3s using pnpm v11.7.0\` (exit 0)
- \`pnpm lint\` ✅ exit 0
- \`pnpm test\` ✅ \`Tasks: 21 successful, 21 total — Time: 40.108s\` (exit 0)
- Local validation ran on Node 24.14.0 — the same version CI will now use

Fixes ENG-1676

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import json, re
src = open('turbo.json').read()
t = json.loads(src)
# fix passThroughEnv: remove misplaced entry
pt = t['tasks']['build']['passThroughEnv']
pt.remove('IMAGES_ALLOW_ALL_HTTPS_HOSTS')
# add to build.env, keeping rough alphabetical placement
env = t['tasks']['build']['env']
for var in ('IMAGES_ALLOW_ALL_HTTPS_HOSTS', 'PUBLIC_URL'):
    if var not in env:
        # insert before first entry that sorts after it, else append
        idx = next((i for i, e in enumerate(env) if e > var), len(env))
        env.insert(idx, var)
json.dump(t, open('turbo.json','w'), indent=2)
open('turbo.json','a').write('\n')
print('env has flag:', 'IMAGES_ALLOW_ALL_HTTPS_HOSTS' in t['tasks']['build']['env'], '| env has PUBLIC_URL:', 'PUBLIC_URL' in t['tasks']['build']['env'])
EOF

input.command:
gh pr create --title "fix(web): stop next/image from optimizing arbitrary remote hosts" --body "## Problem

\`next.config.mjs\` builds a curated \`images.remotePatterns\` allowlist (GitHub/Slack/Google avatars, Unsplash, the cloud CDN bucket, …) and then **unconditionally appends** \`{ protocol: \"https\", hostname: \"**\" }\` — making the allowlist decorative. The image optimizer would fetch and process **any** HTTPS URL: an open image proxy (bandwidth/CPU abuse), an SSRF-adjacent surface, and an optimizer-cache poisoning vector. Doubly relevant with \`dangerouslyAllowSVG: true\`.

## Solution

- Removed the unconditional wildcard.
- Instance-specific hosts are now **derived from the environment** and deduped against the static allowlist: \`WEBAPP_URL\`, \`PUBLIC_URL\`, \`S3_ENDPOINT_URL\`.
- **Uploaded files are unaffected**: they are served from same-origin \`/storage/...\` paths, which never consult \`remotePatterns\`.
- Escape hatch for deployments that render user-provided image URLs from arbitrary external hosts: \`IMAGES_ALLOW_ALL_HTTPS_HOSTS=1\` restores the previous behavior (documented in \`.env.example\` with an explicit open-proxy warning; registered in turbo's \`build.env\` so cached builds can't go stale — the ENG-1663 guard test enforced this, nice).

### ⚠️ Review callout for Cloud

If Formbricks Cloud relies on arbitrary external image URLs (e.g. pasted logo URLs on unknown domains), those will stop rendering through the optimizer until either the host is allowlisted or \`IMAGES_ALLOW_ALL_HTTPS_HOSTS=1\` is set for the Cloud deployment. Secure-by-default seemed right for the OSS default; happy to flip the default if you prefer.

\`dangerouslyAllowSVG\` is intentionally left unchanged here — worth its own look as part of ENG-1691 (CSP hardening).

## Validation

- Functional smoke test (config loaded with real env):
  - default: \`wildcard present: false\`, derived hosts deduped ✅
  - \`PUBLIC_URL=https://surveys.example.org S3_ENDPOINT_URL=https://minio.internal:9000\` → both hosts appended ✅
  - \`IMAGES_ALLOW_ALL_HTTPS_HOSTS=1\` → \`wildcard present: true\` ✅
- \`pnpm install\` ✅ exit 0
- \`pnpm lint\` ✅ exit 0
- \`pnpm test\` ✅ \`Tasks: 21 successful, 21 total — Time: 42.351s\` (includes the turbo-build-env guard test, which caught and enforced the env bookkeeping during development)

Fixes ENG-1678

input.command:
gh pr create --title "chore(vitest): remove dead environmentMatchGlobs, adopt projects layout" --body "## Problem

\`environmentMatchGlobs\` was **removed in Vitest 4**, but is still configured in \`apps/web/vite.config.mts\` and \`packages/surveys/vite.config.mts\` — silently ignored. Component tests only pass because each file carries a \`// @vitest-environment\` pragma (the surveys config even references \`jsdom\`, which isn't installed there — proof it never ran under v4). The next \`.test.tsx\` added without a pragma runs in a node environment and fails with confusing \`document is not defined\` errors. The root \`vitest.workspace.ts\` also used the deprecated workspace-file approach.

## Solution

- **apps/web**: \`unit\` (node) + \`components\` (jsdom) projects. \`*.test.tsx\` get jsdom automatically — pragma removed from \`use-sign-out.test.tsx\` to prove it.
- **packages/surveys**: \`surveys-unit\` (node) + \`surveys-components\` (happy-dom) projects, mirroring the effective pragma reality. Pragmas removed from both \`.test.tsx\` files; the handful of DOM-dependent \`.ts\` tests keep their per-file pragma.
- **jsdom** added as an explicit \`apps/web\` devDependency — it was a phantom dep resolved only via hoisting (ENG-1681's sibling problem).
- **Root \`vitest.workspace.ts\` deleted, deliberately without a root replacement.** I verified empirically that a root \`projects\` list referencing package configs **silently discards their nested project definitions** — the jsdom test ran in node from the root (\`environment 0ms\`) while passing per-package. A root config would therefore be a trap that mis-runs web/surveys tests. Nothing uses root-level vitest: CI and dev run per package via \`turbo run test\`, and IDE extensions auto-discover per-package configs (where the projects work correctly). Deviation from the issue's AC noted here for review.

## Validation

- Per-package: \`packages/surveys\` → 26 files / 650 tests ✅; \`apps/web\` → 503 files ✅ (identical counts to before — no tests lost by the include/exclude split)
- De-pragma'd jsdom/happy-dom tests pass via project env selection ✅
- \`pnpm install\` ✅ exit 0 · \`pnpm lint\` ✅ exit 0 · \`pnpm test\` ✅ \`Tasks: 21 successful, 21 total — Time: 42.101s\`

Fixes ENG-1680

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import re, pathlib

files = [
    ".github/workflows/test.yml",
    ".github/workflows/lint.yml",
    ".github/workflows/sonarqube.yml",
    ".github/workflows/integration-tests.yml",
    ".github/workflows/translation-check.yml",
    ".github/workflows/api-v3-spec.yml",
    ".github/workflows/e2e.yml",
    ".github/actions/cache-build-web/action.yml",
]

# Swap "Setup Node.js" and "Install pnpm" steps, add cache: pnpm to setup-node.
# Pattern is indentation-agnostic within each file.
pat = re.compile(
    r"(?P<node>(?P<ind>[ ]+)- name: Setup Node\.js[^\n]*\n"
    r"(?:(?P=ind)  [^\n]*\n)+?)"
    r"(?P<blank>\n?)"
    r"(?P<pnpm>(?P=ind)- name: Install pnpm\n"
    r"(?:(?P=ind)  [^\n]*\n)+?)"
    , re.M)

for f in files:
    p = pathlib.Path(f)
    s = p.read_text()
    m = pat.search(s)
    if not m:
        print(f"NO MATCH: {f}")
        continue
    node = m.group("node")
    # add cache: pnpm under the with: block of setup-node (after node-version line)
    node_cached = re.sub(r"(\n([ ]+)node-version: [^\n]+\n)", r"\1\2cache: pnpm\n", node, count=1)
    if node_cached == node:
        print(f"CACHE NOT ADDED: {f}")
        continue
    replacement = m.group("pnpm") + "\n" + node_cached
    s = s[: m.start()] + replacement + s[m.end():]
    p.write_text(s)
    print(f"OK: {f}")
EOF

input.command:
head -12 .github/workflows/sonarqube.yml; echo "==="; python3 - <<'EOF'
import pathlib

env_block = """
env:
  TURBO_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.TURBO_TOKEN }}
  TURBO_TEAM: ${{ vars.TURBO_TEAM }}
"""

for f in [".github/workflows/test.yml", ".github/workflows/lint.yml", ".github/workflows/sonarqube.yml"]:
    p = pathlib.Path(f)
    s = p.read_text()
    if "TURBO_TOKEN" in s:
        print(f"already has TURBO: {f}")
        continue
    # insert workflow-level env after the `on:` block (before `permissions:` or `jobs:`)
    for anchor in ("\npermissions:", "\njobs:"):
        idx = s.find(anchor)
        if idx != -1:
            s = s[:idx] + env_block + s[idx:]
            break
    p.write_text(s)
    print(f"OK: {f}")
EOF

old = """      - name: Setup Node.js 22.x
        if: steps.harness.outputs.present == 'true'
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020 # v4.4.0
        with:

      - name: Install pnpm
        if: steps.harness.outputs.present == 'true'
        uses: pnpm/action-setup@a7487c7e89a18df4991f7f222e4898a00d66ddda # v4.1.0
"""
new = """      - name: Install pnpm
        if: steps.harness.outputs.present == 'true'
        uses: pnpm/action-setup@a7487c7e89a18df4991f7f222e4898a00d66ddda # v4.1.0

      - name: Setup Node.js 22.x
        if: steps.harness.outputs.present == 'true'
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020 # v4.4.0
        with:
          node-version: 22.x
          cache: pnpm
"""
assert old in s, "integration pattern not found"
p.write_text(s.replace(old, new, 1))

# translation-check.yml: gated on steps.changes.outputs.translations

"""
new = """      - name: Install pnpm

        uses: pnpm/action-setup@a7487c7e89a18df4991f7f222e4898a00d66ddda # v4.1.0

        if: steps.changes.outputs.translations == 'true'
        uses: actions/setup-node@49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020 # v4.4.0
        with:
          node-version: 22.x
          cache: pnpm
"""
assert old in s, "translation pattern not found"
p.write_text(s.replace(old, new, 1))
print("translation-check.yml done")
EOF

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import yaml
class Strict(yaml.SafeLoader): pass
def no_dupes(loader, node, deep=False):
    mapping = {}
    for k_node, v_node in node.value:
        k = loader.construct_object(k_node, deep=deep)
        if k in mapping: raise ValueError(f"duplicate key: {k} at line {k_node.start_mark.line+1}")
        mapping[k] = loader.construct_object(v_node, deep=deep)
    return mapping
Strict.add_constructor(yaml.resolver.BaseResolver.DEFAULT_MAPPING_TAG, no_dupes)
files = [".github/workflows/test.yml", ".github/workflows/lint.yml", ".github/workflows/sonarqube.yml",
         ".github/workflows/integration-tests.yml", ".github/workflows/translation-check.yml",
         ".github/workflows/api-v3-spec.yml", ".github/workflows/e2e.yml",
         ".github/actions/cache-build-web/action.yml"]
ok = True
for f in files:
    try:
        yaml.load(open(f), Strict); print(f"strict-valid: {f}")
    except Exception as e:
        ok = False; print(f"ERROR {f}: {e}")
print("ALL OK" if ok else "FAILURES")
EOF
echo "=== sanity: every touched workflow has pnpm before setup-node with cache ==="; for f in .github/workflows/test.yml .github/workflows/lint.yml .github/workflows/sonarqube.yml .github/workflows/integration-tests.yml .github/workflows/translation-check.yml .github/workflows/api-v3-spec.yml .github/workflows/e2e.yml .github/actions/cache-build-web/action.yml; do pnpm_line=$(grep -n "pnpm/action-setup" $f | head -1 | cut -d: -f1); node_line=$(grep -n "actions/setup-node" $f | head -1 | cut -d: -f1); cache=$(grep -c "cache: pnpm" $f); echo "$f: pnpm@$pnpm_line node@$node_line cache-lines=$cache $([ $pnpm_line -lt $node_line ] && echo OK || echo WRONG-ORDER)"; done

input.command:
gh pr create --title "ci: cache the pnpm store and extend Turbo remote cache to all workflows" --body "## Problem

Two caching gaps burned CI minutes on every PR run:

1. **No pnpm store caching anywhere** — every job of every run did a cold \`pnpm install --frozen-lockfile\` over the full workspace.
2. **Turbo remote cache only partially wired** — \`TURBO_TOKEN\`/\`TURBO_TEAM\` were set in build/e2e/integration workflows but **not** in \`test.yml\`, \`lint.yml\`, or \`sonarqube.yml\`: the highest-frequency jobs always ran turbo cold.

## Solution

- \`pnpm/action-setup\` now runs **before** \`actions/setup-node\` (required — setup-node's cache resolution shells out to \`pnpm store path\`), and \`cache: pnpm\` is enabled in: **test, lint, sonarqube, integration-tests, translation-check, api-v3-spec, e2e**, and the **cache-build-web** composite action. \`chromatic.yml\` already had a manual store cache and is left unchanged.
- \`TURBO_TOKEN\`/\`TURBO_TEAM\` added at workflow level to **test, lint, sonarqube** (test/lint are reusable workflows called with \`secrets: inherit\` from \`pr.yml\`, so the secret flows through; sonarqube runs standalone with direct secret access).
- All \`if:\` step guards preserved (integration-tests/translation-check gate their setup steps — verified per file).
- Action SHA pins unchanged.

## Validation

- All 8 touched files pass **strict YAML validation** including a duplicate-key check.
- Ordering sanity-checked programmatically: pnpm-setup precedes setup-node in every touched file.
- \`pnpm install\` ✅ exit 0 · \`pnpm lint\` ✅ exit 0 · \`pnpm test\` ✅ \`Tasks: 21 successful, 21 total — Time: 43.882s\`
- The real proof is this PR's own CI run — the second run of any job should show the store cache being restored.

**Note:** this will conflict trivially with #8500 (ENG-1676) — both touch the setup-node blocks. Whichever merges second needs a one-line rebase (\`node-version\` → \`node-version-file\` alongside \`cache: pnpm\`).

Fixes ENG-1679

input.command:
cd /Users/matti/Developer/formbricks && python3 - <<'EOF'
import pathlib
# add unusedVarsConvention after the tseslint recommended spread in library.mjs and react.mjs
for f, anchor in [("packages/config-eslint/library.mjs", "  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,\n"),
                  ("packages/config-eslint/react.mjs", "  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,\n")]:
    p = pathlib.Path(f)
    s = p.read_text()
    assert anchor in s, f
    s = s.replace(anchor, anchor + "  unusedVarsConvention,\n", 1)
    # extend the import from base.mjs
    s = s.replace('import { base, commonIgnores } from "./base.mjs";',
                  'import { base, commonIgnores, unusedVarsConvention } from "./base.mjs";')
    p.write_text(s)
    print("updated", f)
EOF

input.prompt:
You are working in /Users/matti/Developer/formbricks on branch matti/eng-1677-migrate-eslint-to-v9-flat-config. The repo was just migrated from ESLint 8 (.eslintrc) to ESLint 9 flat config. Your ONLY job: make `pnpm lint` pass (exit 0) across the whole monorepo, following the policies below exactly. Do not touch anything unrelated to lint.

## Current state
- Shared flat configs live in packages/config-eslint: base.mjs (turbo + prettier + @vitest conventions + unusedVarsConvention export), library.mjs (js+tseslint recommended, node globals — used by ai, cache, i18n-utils, jobs, js-core, logger, storage, types, vite-plugins, database), react.mjs (full react tier — survey-ui), react-hooks.mjs (lite tier — surveys, email), next.mjs (eslint-config-next based — apps/web).
- Each package/app has an eslint.config.mjs importing its tier. Legacy .eslintrc*/.eslintignore files are deleted. Root eslint is 9.39.5.
- First run showed @formbricks/logger failing; other packages not yet measured.

## How to run
- Per package: `cd packages/<name> && pnpm lint` (note: many lint scripts include --fix; that is expected and fine).
- Everything at once: `pnpm turbo lint --continue` from the repo root to see all failures.
- The full suite must finish green: `pnpm lint` at root, exit 0.

## Fix policies (in priority order)
1. **turbo/no-undeclared-env-vars errors**: do NOT edit turbo.json (cache-key semantics are owned elsewhere). Instead, in the affected package's own eslint.config.mjs, configure the rule with an allowList for exactly the vars reported, with a one-line comment, e.g.:
   ```js
   {
     rules: {
       // runtime-only env reads; hashing them in turbo.json is tracked separately (ENG-1682)
       "turbo/no-undeclared-env-vars": ["error", { allowList: ["OTEL_LOGS_ENABLED", "NEXT_PHASE"] }],
     },
   },
   ```
2. **Unused eslint-disable directives** (warnings or errors): delete the stale comment lines. Also delete disable comments referencing rules that no longer exist (e.g. import/*, unicorn/*, @typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-* from the removed Vercel style guide) — ESLint will report these; there are only a handful repo-wide.
3. **@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars for underscore-prefixed names**: already handled by shared config (ignore patterns for ^_). If a NON-underscore genuinely-unused variable fires: if it is trivially dead (unused import, unused local), delete it; if it is a function parameter needed for a signature, rename to _name. Never delete anything with side effects.
4. **Any other rule with ≤5 occurrences in a package**: fix mechanically if it is trivially safe (never change runtime logic or control flow). Otherwise add a `// eslint-disable-next-line <rule> -- migration ENG-1677` comment.
5. **Any rule with >5 occurrences in a package**: do NOT fix files one by one. Turn the rule off in that package's eslint.config.mjs with a comment `// TODO(ENG-1677): enable incrementally` and move on.
6. **NEVER**: change runtime logic, rename exports, edit files under packages/database/migration(s), edit turbo.json, edit package.json dependencies, or edit anything in .github. Do not modify the shared tier files in packages/config-eslint EXCEPT if the identical problem appears in 3+ packages (then prefer one shared fix with a comment).
7. If a package's eslint run CRASHES (config error, missing plugin, parser error), diagnose and fix the config — report what was wrong.

## After lint is green
Run `pnpm test` from the root. It must also pass (your lint fixes must not break tests — if a test fails because of something you changed, fix your change, not the test). If a test failure clearly predates your changes (verify with git stash), report it instead of fixing.

## Report back (final message = data for me, not prose for the user)
- Per package: what categories of problems were found and what you did (counts).
- Every rule you turned OFF via policy 5, per package.
- Every allowList entry added (policy 1).
- Any files where you added disable comments (policy 4).
- Anything that crashed and how you fixed it.
- Final exit codes of `pnpm lint` and `pnpm test`.

input.command:
git commit -m "chore(eslint): migrate to ESLint 9 flat config

ESLint 8 is EOL (2024-10-05) and the eslintrc format blocked plugin
upgrades. Replaces the @vercel/style-guide based shared config (no
flat-config support) and the duplicated legacy-* variants with flat
tiers in @formbricks/eslint-config:

- base.mjs: turbo + prettier + @vitest test-over-it convention +
  shared unused-vars underscore convention
- library.mjs: eslint + typescript-eslint recommended (node packages)
- react.mjs: + react/react-hooks recommended (survey-ui)
- react-hooks.mjs: lite tier mirroring legacy-react (surveys, email)
- next.mjs: eslint-config-next 16 flat + legacy-next rule parity
  (apps/web)

All 15 packages/apps now carry a small eslint.config.mjs; every
.eslintrc*/.eslintignore is deleted, including the root ones.
database moves from the (mis-assigned) Next config to library.
--ext/--config flags removed from lint scripts (unsupported in flat
mode). eslint 8.57.1 -> 9.39.5.

Fallout handled without touching runtime logic: stale disable
directives removed, turbo/no-undeclared-env-vars allowLists for
runtime-only reads, and the new react-hooks v7 compiler-era rules
(set-state-in-effect etc., ~180 hits) disabled in apps/web with
TODO(ENG-1677) markers for incremental adoption.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" | tail -3 && git push -u origin matti/eng-1677-migrate-eslint-to-v9-flat-config 2>&1 | tail -2

gh pr create --title "chore(eslint): migrate to ESLint 9 flat config" --body "## Problem

ESLint 8 reached end-of-life on 2024-10-05 — no security or bug fixes — and the legacy \`.eslintrc\` format increasingly locks us out of plugin upgrades. The shared config was built on \`@vercel/style-guide\` (no flat-config support, effectively stalled) and carried two drifting generations of configs (\`library/react/next\` + \`legacy-*\`), with the app itself linted by the older one.

## Solution

**New flat tiers in \`@formbricks/eslint-config\`** (ESLint \`9.39.5\`, \`typescript-eslint\` 8.63):

| Tier | Contents | Used by |
|---|---|---|
| \`base\` | turbo + prettier + \`@vitest\` test-over-it + underscore unused-vars convention | (composed by all) |
| \`library\` | eslint + typescript-eslint recommended, node globals | ai, cache, i18n-utils, jobs, js-core, logger, storage, types, vite-plugins, **database** |
| \`react\` | + react/react-hooks recommended | survey-ui |
| \`react-hooks\` | lite tier mirroring \`legacy-react\` | surveys, email |
| \`next\` | \`eslint-config-next\` 16 (flat-native) + legacy-next rule parity | apps/web |

gh pr edit 8498 --body "## Problem

\`pnpm-workspace.yaml\` declares a \`minimumReleaseAgeExclude\` for \`@formbricks/hub\` but never sets \`minimumReleaseAge\` itself. On pnpm 11 a **default 1-day cooldown** applies, but the effective policy was implicit, undocumented, and weaker than intended for a security-conscious repo — and nothing pinned it against upstream default changes.

*(Correction from review: the original description claimed the cooldown was fully inactive — that was true for pnpm 10 but not for pnpm 11, which defaults \`minimumReleaseAge\` to 1440 minutes. The PR now frames this accurately.)*

## Solution

- \`minimumReleaseAge: 4320\` (3 days) set explicitly — raising the implicit 1-day default; most npm supply-chain compromises are detected and yanked within 1–2 days.
- \`trustLockfile: false\` pinned explicitly (pnpm's current default): \`pnpm install\` **re-verifies entries already in the lockfile** against the cooldown, so a poisoned or bot-written lockfile bump younger than 3 days fails install until it ages or is excluded. Pinning it documents this as a deliberate choice and protects against a future upstream default flip.
- Comment block documents the real pnpm 11 semantics, including the operational consequence: dependency-bump PRs whose lockfile entries are younger than 3 days will fail CI install until the packages age (that is the feature working). The existing \`@formbricks/hub\` name-based exclusion (first-party, published by our own CI) is unchanged.

content.text:
The finding was **valid** — verified against current pnpm docs and fixed on the branch ([88afe55](https://github.com/formbricks/formbricks/pull/8498/commits)). It actually exposed two inaccuracies, one of which undercut the PR's original premise:

1. **pnpm 11 defaults `minimumReleaseAge` to 1440 min (1 day)** — so the cooldown was never fully inactive, as both my audit and the PR claimed. My earlier `pnpm config get minimumReleaseAge` returning `undefined` only showed the *configured* value, not the effective default. The PR's real value is raising 1 day → 3 days and making the policy explicit.
2. **"Existing lockfile entries are unaffected" was wrong** — pnpm 11 re-verifies loaded-lockfile entries against the cooldown during install unless `trustLockfile: true`. That re-verification is exactly what catches poisoned or bot-written lockfile bumps, so per the reviewer's suggestion I pinned the intended behavior explicitly: `trustLockfile: false`, with a comment spelling out the consequence — a dependency-bump PR whose lockfile entries are younger than 3 days will fail CI install until the packages age (that's the feature working, not a bug).

Validation on the updated branch: normal **and** `--frozen-lockfile` installs pass with re-verification active against the current lockfile, `pnpm lint` and the full test suite are green. I also rewrote the [PR description](https://github.com/formbricks/formbricks/pull/8498) so it no longer sells the wrong premise, and left a comment on the PR documenting the verification outcome.

One operational note for the team before merging: with the 3-day cooldown + lockfile re-verification, Renovate/Dependabot PRs for freshly published versions will sit red for up to 3 days. If that friction is unwanted, the knobs are shortening `minimumReleaseAge` or adding targeted excludes — I'd keep 3 days and let bump PRs age.

content.text:
Verified empirically (not just from docs). Here's exactly what happens.

**Short answer:** Yes — if Dependabot bumps a dependency to a version published less than 3 days ago, your CI `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` will **fail with exit 1** on that PR. I just reproduced the mechanism: with re-verification active (`trustLockfile: false`), pnpm re-checks the age of the versions written in the lockfile on *every* install, frozen or not, and exits non-zero if any is younger than the cutoff. It's not a warning — it's a hard failure.

**The concrete Dependabot flow:**
1. Dependabot opens a PR bumping `foo` 1.0.0 → 1.2.0 and writes 1.2.0 into `pnpm-lock.yaml`. Dependabot writes the lockfile in its own environment — it doesn't enforce our cooldown, so the PR gets created fine.
2. CI runs `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`. pnpm sees `foo@1.2.0` in the lockfile was published, say, 6 hours ago → **install fails, check goes red.**
3. The PR is blocked until either: the version crosses the 3-day mark (just **re-run CI, no code change** — it then passes), you add `foo` to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`, or you lower the global threshold.

If Dependabot runs on a weekly schedule against a package that released more than 3 days before the run, there's no failure at all — the version is already old enough.

**Two things that make this less alarming than it sounds:**
- **It's self-healing and temporary.** Nothing is permanently broken; the PR just can't merge until the release ages past 3 days. That's the feature — it's the window where a compromised release typically gets caught and yanked.
- **It's not brand-new behavior I introduced.** pnpm 11 already defaults `minimumReleaseAge` to 1 day with lockfile re-verification on, so today on `main` a Dependabot bump of a <1-day-old package would *already* fail CI. My PR widens that window from 1 day to 3 days — same mechanism, longer delay.

**So it's a real tradeoff, and it's your call:**

| Option | Effect |
|---|---|
| Keep 3 days (current PR) | Strongest protection; dependency-bump PRs may sit red up to 3 days |
| Drop to ~1 day (`1440`) | Matches pnpm's default; much less Dependabot friction, smaller safety window |
| Keep 3 days + exclude the bots' churny deps | Targeted, but a maintenance list to keep |

My recommendation is to keep 3 days and just let bump PRs age — the friction is "a red check that goes green on re-run in a few days," which is cheap insurance against exactly the supply-chain attacks this setting exists for. But if your team merges Dependabot PRs aggressively and that red check will be a constant nuisance, dropping to 1 day (the pnpm default) is the reasonable middle ground.

Want me to change the value, or leave it at 3 days? I haven't touched anything — the branch is still at `4320` and the working tree is clean.

content.text:
Here's what that PR comment is really about. This is the deepest of the seven, so it's worth understanding properly.

## What "hoisted" means

pnpm can arrange `node_modules` two ways:

- **Isolated (pnpm's default):** every package sees *only the dependencies it declared in its own `package.json`*. Transitive dependencies (the deps of your deps) are tucked away in `node_modules/.pnpm` and symlinked so they aren't directly importable. If you `import` something you didn't declare, you get `Cannot find module` — immediately, loudly.
- **Hoisted (what this repo uses):** everything — your deps *and* all the transitive deps — gets flattened into one big top-level `node_modules`, npm-style. Node's module resolution walks up the directory tree and finds anything sitting in that flat pile, whether or not the package that's importing it ever declared it.

The analogy: isolated is everyone having their own labeled toolbox with only the tools they signed out. Hoisted is one giant shared bench where you can grab any tool that happens to be lying there — convenient, until someone reorganizes the bench and the tool you were quietly relying on is gone.

That "quietly relying on" is the problem, and it has a name: **phantom dependencies**. A package works today because something it needs got hoisted to the root (often as a transitive dep of a *different* package), even though it never declared it. It keeps working right up until an unrelated version bump removes or moves that package, and then it breaks — at build or runtime, in a way that's confusing because nothing in that package's own `package.json` explains the failure.

## The two concrete offenders in *this* repo

This isn't hypothetical — the PR names the two live examples, and I just confirmed both are still true on the branch:

1. **`@formbricks/js-core` (ENG-1681):** its `package.json` declares **zero** runtime dependencies, yet its build and type config reach straight across package boundaries — `tsconfig.json` pulls in `../types/surveys.d.ts` and `../database/src/index.ts`, and `vite.config.ts` imports `../../apps/web/package.json`. None of `types`, `database`, or the web app is declared as a dependency. The hidden edges mean **Turbo can't know js-core depends on `database`** — so a change in `database` won't rebuild js-core or bust its cache. (This is the concrete cost that ties back to your caching PR.)

2. **`react` / `react-dom` in the *root* `package.json` (ENG-1693):** a workspace root should carry only tooling, but this root declares `react` and `react-dom` as runtime deps. They're there purely to *force* a single React copy to hoist to the top so every package and the app resolve the same one — a workaround that only makes sense because the layout is hoisted.

## Why we can't flip to isolated right now

If you flip `nodeLinker: hoisted` → isolated today, every phantom dependency turns into a hard `Cannot find module` at once, and the build breaks in a cascade. Worse: **hoisting hides the full list** of what's affected. We know about js-core and the root React because the audit found them, but there are almost certainly more — the only way to enumerate them completely is to flip the linker and see everything that breaks. So the flip is simultaneously the *discovery tool* and the *thing that can't be merged until the discoveries are fixed*. You can't land a one-shot change that red-lights CI.

## What reaching the long-term goal actually takes

The path, in order:

1. **Make each package declare what it uses.** For js-core: add `@formbricks/types`, `@formbricks/database` as `workspace:*` deps, and stop importing `apps/web/package.json` (pass the version in some other way). That's ENG-1681.
2. **Remove the root-level runtime deps** once each consumer declares its own React. That's ENG-1693.
3. **Repeat for whatever else surfaces** — likely a short iterative loop: flip the linker on a scratch branch, collect the `Cannot find module` errors, add the missing declarations, repeat until clean.
4. **Flip `nodeLinker` to isolated** (or just delete the line, since isolated is pnpm's default) and land it. That's the payoff step, tracked on ENG-1674.

The payoff is that the dependency graph becomes *honest*: a package can only import what it declares, Turbo can order builds and invalidate caches correctly, and upgrades/extractions stop being minefields.

## So what does *this* PR (#8499) actually do?

Deliberately, only the safe part: it removes two settings that were **doing nothing** (`legacyPeerDeps`, which isn't even a real pnpm option, and `shamefullyHoist`, which only affects the isolated linker and is redundant under `hoisted`), and it *documents* why `hoisted` is staying for now. It intentionally does **not** touch `nodeLinker`, because that flip is the big migration above. I verified the `node_modules` layout is byte-for-byte unchanged after this PR — it's a comment-and-cleanup change, zero behavioral risk, while the real migration stays scoped to ENG-1674.

One nuance worth flagging so the comment isn't misread: js-core's reach-ins are *relative-path* imports, which technically resolve regardless of the linker — so that specific case is less "isolated linker will break it" and more "undeclared coupling that Turbo is blind to." The cleanest pure-hoisting example is the root React. Both are the same underlying disease (undeclared edges), which is why they're prerequisites for the flip. Happy to walk through the js-core fix (ENG-1681) concretely if you want to see what step 1 looks like in practice.

gh pr edit 8499 --title "chore(pnpm): remove legacyPeerDeps no-op, document node-linker decision" --body "## Problem

\`pnpm-workspace.yaml\` carried \`legacyPeerDeps: true\` — an **npm** option that pnpm does not recognize (not in pnpm's settings docs); a silent no-op carried over from an npm-era config. And the reason the repo runs \`nodeLinker: hoisted\` at all was undocumented.

## Solution

- Removed the \`legacyPeerDeps\` no-op.
- Documented the \`nodeLinker: hoisted\` decision inline: it stays for now because packages still rely on hoisted undeclared deps (ENG-1681: js-core cross-package reach-ins; ENG-1693: root react/react-dom); the migration to the strict \`isolated\` linker is tracked on ENG-1674 and attempted separately.
- **\`shamefullyHoist\` is intentionally left in place** (earlier revision of this PR removed it). It only takes effect under the isolated linker, so it belongs with that migration, not this cleanup — keeps this PR a pure no-op removal with zero behavioral change.

**Layout verified unchanged:** full \`node_modules\` purge + reinstall produces the same flat tree; \`pnpm-lock.yaml\` has zero diff.

## Validation

- \`pnpm install\` (full purge + reinstall) ✅ exit 0
- \`pnpm lint\` ✅ exit 0
- \`pnpm test\` ✅ (unchanged from prior run: 21/21 tasks)

The isolated-linker migration itself is being attempted on a separate branch — results will inform ENG-1674.

content.text: The isolated layout is live (react is now symlinked, zod is gone from root), and the build immediately surfaced the first phantom-dep layer: **`tsc: command not found`** across 7 packages. That's the migration working exactly as predicted — those packages invoke `tsc` in their build scripts but never declared `typescript`; under hoisting it was on the PATH from the root, under isolation it isn't. Let me map the full breakage surface before fixing:

content.text: Uniform and clean: every package invokes `tsc` but only `config-typescript` declares `typescript` — so the fix is to declare `typescript@5.9.3` (the canonical pin) in each of the 15. Applying that:

input.command:
cd /Users/matti/Developer/formbricks
python3 - <<'EOF'
import json, pathlib
targets = ["packages/ai","packages/cache","packages/database","packages/email","packages/i18n-utils",
           "packages/jobs","packages/js-core","packages/logger","packages/storage","packages/survey-ui",
           "packages/surveys","packages/types","packages/vite-plugins","apps/web","apps/storybook"]
VER = "5.9.3"
for t in targets:
    p = pathlib.Path(t)/"package.json"
    d = json.loads(p.read_text())
    dev = d.setdefault("devDependencies", {})
    if "typescript" in dev:
        print("already has:", t); continue
    dev["typescript"] = VER
    d["devDependencies"] = dict(sorted(dev.items()))
    p.write_text(json.dumps(d, indent=2) + "\n")
    print("added typescript to:", t)
EOF

content.text: This is now a genuinely large burn-down — and notice the web app didn't even build because its dependency `@formbricks/surveys` failed first (the dependency chain cascades). The scope is exactly what "why we can't do it now" predicted. Rather than hand-fix dozens of declarations across the tree, I'll delegate the systematic sweep to a subagent with tight guardrails — its job is to push to green (or pinpoint what's unfixable), especially answering whether the **web app** builds under isolated.

input.description: Burn down isolated-linker phantom deps
input.prompt:
You are on branch `matti/eng-1674-migrate-to-isolated-node-linker` in /Users/matti/Developer/formbricks. We are migrating pnpm from the flat `hoisted` node-linker to the strict `isolated` linker (pnpm default). Under isolation a package can only import/use what it declares in its own package.json, so all the "phantom dependencies" that previously worked via hoisting now fail. Your job: **fix every phantom dependency by declaring the real dependency**, iterating until the workspace is green — or precisely identify what cannot be fixed by a dependency declaration.

## Already done (do not redo)
- `pnpm-workspace.yaml`: `nodeLinker: isolated`, no `shamefullyHoist`, no `legacyPeerDeps`.
- `typescript@5.9.3` added as devDep to all 15 packages/apps that run `tsc`.
- `prettier@3.8.3` + `@trivago/prettier-plugin-sort-imports@6.0.2` + `prettier-plugin-tailwindcss@0.7.4` + `prettier-plugin-sort-json@4.2.0` added to the ROOT package.json (lint-staged runs prettier at root).
- node_modules is currently in isolated layout.

## The core loop
1. Run the failing task (`pnpm build`, `pnpm turbo build --continue`, `pnpm lint`, `pnpm test`, or per-package `pnpm --filter <pkg> build`).
2. For each failure that is a **resolution failure**, add the missing dependency to the OFFENDING package's package.json and re-install with `CI=true pnpm install` (CI=true is REQUIRED — without it pnpm refuses to purge node_modules in this non-TTY shell).
3. Repeat until green or stuck.

## What counts as a phantom dep to FIX (these are caused by the migration)
- `sh: <bin>: command not found` (e.g. `cross-env`, `tsc`) → the package's build script uses a CLI it didn't declare.
- `Cannot find module 'X'` / `Could not find a declaration file for module 'X'` → undeclared runtime or types dependency.
- `Cannot find namespace 'NodeJS'` → missing `@types/node`.
- `error TS7016 ... react/jsx-runtime ... implicitly 'any'` and cascading `TS7031 implicitly any` in the same file → missing `react`/`@types/react` (the TS7031s usually vanish once the JSX types resolve).

## Known first batch (verify by reading each package's imports, then declare)
- `packages/email`: needs `react`, `react-dom`, `@types/react`, `@types/react-dom`, `lucide-react`, `@react-email/render` (confirm against its actual imports — some may come transitively via `@react-email/components`).
- `packages/surveys`: needs `cross-env`.
- `packages/js-core`: needs `@types/node`.
Expect MORE to surface in typecheck/lint/test after these.

## Version selection (critical — match the rest of the repo, never invent)
Reuse the version already used elsewhere. Known canonical versions in this repo:
- `@types/node`: `25.4.0`, `@types/react`: `19.2.14`, `@types/react-dom`: `19.2.3` (all in packages/config-typescript/package.json)
- `react`/`react-dom`: `19.2.6`; `lucide-react`: `0.577.0`; `cross-env`: `10.1.0` (all in apps/web/package.json)
- For anything else, grep other package.json files or inspect `node_modules/.pnpm` for the resolved version and reuse it exactly (the repo pins exact versions — no `^`).
Put type packages and build-tool CLIs in `devDependencies`; runtime imports in `dependencies`.

## Pre-existing failure — DO NOT chase, but handle correctly
`@formbricks/email`'s tsc emits `TS2550: Property 'includes'/'flat' does not exist ... change the 'lib' compiler option to es2016/es2019` for files under `../types/`. **This fails on `main` too** (verified) — it is NOT caused by the migration; it's a stale `lib: ["ES2015","DOM"]` in `packages/config-typescript/react-library.json`. If, after you declare email's phantom deps, the ONLY remaining email errors are these `lib`/es2016/es2019 ones, that is acceptable — but to get a clean green you MAY bump that shared config's `lib` to `["ES2020","DOM"]` (ES2020 includes Array.includes/flat and is safe for this toolchain). If you do, flag it clearly as a deviation and re-run survey-ui (the other react-library consumer) to confirm nothing breaks.

## THE most important question
`@formbricks/web` (Next.js) is the priority. In an earlier `--continue` run it emitted `internal error: entered unreachable code: there must be a path to a root` — but that was while its workspace dependencies (surveys, js-core) were failing to build, so web may not have had valid inputs. Once ALL of web's workspace deps build cleanly, run `pnpm build --filter=@formbricks/web` and determine definitively: **does the web app build under the isolated linker, yes or no?** If it still panics, dig in (it may need a dep declared, or it may be a real incompatibility between Next/turbopack and the isolated symlink layout). Report the definitive answer with evidence.

## Hard constraints
- ONLY edit `package.json` files to add dependency declarations (plus, optionally, the single `react-library.json` `lib` bump described above). 
- Do NOT edit source code logic, turbo.json, database migrations (`packages/database/migration*/`), `.github/`, or the shared eslint configs.
- Do NOT touch the untracked `apps/agents/` directory or `MONOREPO_CONFIG_AUDIT.md`.
- Do NOT commit or push anything — leave all changes in the working tree.
- If a failure genuinely cannot be fixed by declaring a dependency (needs a source change), STOP on that one and report it — do not hack around it.

## Final validation + report
Run, in order, and capture true exit codes (use `set -o pipefail` if piping): `CI=true pnpm install`, then `pnpm lint`, `pnpm test`, `pnpm build`.
Report back (this is data for me, not prose for an end user):
1. A table: every dependency added, which package, which section (dep/devDep), version, and the error it fixed.
2. Whether you did the react-library.json lib bump, and why.
3. Final exit codes for install / lint / test / build.
4. **The definitive web-build verdict** with the evidence.
5. Any remaining failures, classified as (a) pre-existing (also on main) or (b) unfixable-by-declaration, with the specific error.
6. Total count of packages that needed changes.

content.text:
Both parts are done. Here's what I did and the verdict.

## Part A — PR #8499 trimmed
[#8499](https://github.com/formbricks/formbricks/pull/8499) now **leaves `shamefullyHoist` in place** and removes only the genuine no-op (`legacyPeerDeps`), with a comment noting `shamefullyHoist` is handled together with the linker migration. Pushed, re-validated (install/lint green), PR title/body updated.

## Part B — isolated-linker spike (branch `matti/eng-1674-migrate-to-isolated-node-linker`, from main)

I flipped `nodeLinker: hoisted → isolated`, then trial-and-errored through every failure. **The layout is genuinely isolated** (react/zod are no longer flat at the root — they're symlinked through `.pnpm`).

### Does it work? Partially — and the answer is very informative.

**11 of 12 build tasks pass.** Every workspace *package* builds cleanly under isolated once its real dependencies are declared. The phantom deps the migration forced into the open:

| Category | Examples | Packages |
|---|---|---|
| Build-tool CLIs | `typescript` (×15), `cross-env`, `prettier`+3 plugins (root) | most |
| Type packages | `@types/node`, `@types/react`, `@types/react-dom` | js-core, email, … |
| Undeclared **app** runtime deps | `ai`, `jose`, `yaml`, `dompurify`, `@lexical/{html,selection,utils}`, `@otplib/*`, `@formkit/auto-animate`, `@radix-ui/react-visually-hidden`, `@better-auth/utils`, `lucide-react` | apps/web |
| Undeclared package deps | `react`, `react-dom`, `@react-email/render`, `preact`, storybook pkgs | email, surveys, storybook |

Every one was fixable by **declaring the real dependency** — which is exactly the "make the graph honest" payoff. So for the packages, isolated *does* solve the phantom-dependency problem.

### The blocker: the web app won't build under isolated

`@formbricks/web` fails — but **not** on a phantom dep (all deps resolve, everything else builds). It's a **Turbopack internal panic**:

```
internal error: entered unreachable code: there must be a path to a root
  turbopack-core/src/module_graph/mod.rs:662  (ModuleGraphImportTracer)
```

Turbopack's import tracer crashes walking pnpm's **symlinked** `node_modules`. This is an upstream Turbopack limitation with the isolated layout, not something we can fix by declaring dependencies. And since pnpm's `nodeLinker` is workspace-global (no per-package override), this one app blocks the whole flip.

### So: does this solve the problem?

- **Conceptually, yes** — isolated is the right end state and the package-level work is all valid.
- **In practice, not today** — blocked by Turbopack + pnpm-isolated-symlinks. This is a sharper, more concrete version of the "why we can't do it now" note in #8499: it's not just "undeclared deps everywhere" (those are fixable and now fixed), it's that **even with every dep declared, Next 16's default bundler panics on the isolated layout.**

### What I'd recommend (didn't do without your call)

1. **Salvage the valuable half now, on hoisted.** The dependency declarations are correct and beneficial *even under `hoisted`* — they make the graph honest and let Turbo see real edges. I'd extract them into normal PRs for **ENG-1681** (js-core) and **ENG-1693** (root react/react-dom), plus a sweep for the rest, all while keeping `nodeLinker: hoisted`. Safe, incremental, and it makes the eventual flip a one-liner.
2. **Track the Turbopack blocker.** Try `next build --webpack` on the isolated branch (webpack is the non-default escape hatch — I found it but didn't run it; it's another ~1 h build) to confirm the panic is Turbopack-specific, and file/track it upstream. The flip becomes viable once Turbopack handles symlinked `node_modules` (or if you accept a webpack build).
3. **Sequencing:** do the isolated flip *after* the ESLint-9 flat-config PR (#8505) lands — I hit a `types` lint failure on this branch that's purely the old type-aware ESLint choking on incomplete type resolution; it disappears entirely under the flat config.

The spike is committed on the branch (nothing pushed, no PR — it can't merge as-is). `apps/agents/` was left untouched/untracked throughout. Want me to (a) turn the dependency declarations into the ENG-1681/1693 PRs on hoisted, (b) run the `--webpack` experiment to pin down the Turbopack blocker, or (c) push the spike branch as a draft for your team to see?
you are nearly there. can you already open a draft PR for this. then we can revisit after we merged the other PRs.

input.command:
gh pr create --draft --title "spike(pnpm): migrate to isolated node-linker (ENG-1674) — BLOCKED on Turbopack" --body "> **Draft / spike — do not merge yet.** Opened for visibility; revisit after the other config PRs (#8498–#8505) land. Sequencing matters (see below).

## What this is

The real attempt at ENG-1674: flip pnpm from the flat \`hoisted\` node-linker to the strict \`isolated\` (default) linker, so a package can only import what it declares. This is the change that makes the dependency graph honest and lets Turbo order builds/caches correctly.

## What happened (trial-and-error results)

Flipped \`nodeLinker: hoisted → isolated\` (dropped \`shamefullyHoist\`/\`legacyPeerDeps\`), then fixed every failure the isolated linker surfaced. The layout is genuinely isolated (react/zod no longer flat at the root).

**✅ 11 / 12 build tasks pass.** Every workspace *package* builds once its real (previously phantom) dependencies are declared:

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| Build CLIs | \`typescript\` (×15 packages), \`cross-env\`, \`prettier\`+3 plugins (root) |
| Type pkgs | \`@types/node\`, \`@types/react\`, \`@types/react-dom\` |
| Undeclared **web** runtime deps | \`ai\`, \`jose\`, \`yaml\`, \`dompurify\`, \`@lexical/{html,selection,utils}\`, \`@otplib/*\`, \`@formkit/auto-animate\`, \`@radix-ui/react-visually-hidden\`, \`@better-auth/utils\`, \`lucide-react\` |
| Package deps | \`react\`, \`react-dom\`, \`@react-email/render\`, \`preact\`, storybook pkgs |

All fixable by declaring the real dependency — i.e. isolated **does** solve the phantom-dep problem for the packages.

## ❌ The blocker — \`@formbricks/web\` won't build under isolated

Not a phantom dep (all deps resolve, everything else builds). Turbopack panics traversing pnpm's symlinked \`node_modules\`:

\`\`\`
internal error: entered unreachable code: there must be a path to a root
  turbopack-core/src/module_graph/mod.rs:662  (ModuleGraphImportTracer / TurbopackInternalError)
\`\`\`

This is an upstream Turbopack limitation with the isolated symlink layout, and since pnpm's \`nodeLinker\` is workspace-global (no per-package override), it blocks the whole flip.

## Recommended path (to discuss)

1. **Salvage now on \`hoisted\`:** the dependency declarations in this branch are correct and beneficial *without* flipping the linker — extract them into ENG-1681 (js-core) + ENG-1693 (root react) + a sweep for the rest, keeping \`nodeLinker: hoisted\`. Makes the eventual flip a one-liner.
2. **Track the Turbopack blocker:** confirm with \`next build --webpack\` (webpack is the non-default escape hatch) and file/track upstream. Flip becomes viable once Turbopack handles symlinked \`node_modules\`.
3. **Sequence after #8505 (ESLint 9 flat config):** this branch (cut from main) still has the old type-aware ESLint, which throws a \`types\` \`no-unsafe-*\` cascade on incomplete type resolution under isolated; that disappears under flat config.

## Validation

Links: [{"title":"Turbopack ignores turbopack.root in pnpm monorepo, infers App Router `app/` as project directory · Issue #92540 · vercel/next.js","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/92540"},{"title":"Turbopack fails to resolve next/package.json with pnpm v11 enableGlobalVirtualStore · Issue #93556 · vercel/next.js","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/93556"},{"title":"next.config.js: turbopack | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack"},{"title":"[Turbopack] Error: Next.js package not found · vercel/next.js · Discussion #55987","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/55987"},{"title":"Next.js 16.2.4 fails to start web in pnpm monorepo (Turbopack root detection bug) · Issue #189 · nexu-io/open-design","url":"https://github.com/nexu-io/open-design/issues/189"},{"title":"Turbopack can't locate serverExternalPackages if installed via pnpm and are child dependencies of other installed modules · vercel/next.js · Discussion #76247","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/76247"},{"title":"Turbopack Internal Error - Failed to write page endpoint due to FileSystemPath leaving filesystem root · Issue #10703 · vercel/turborepo","url":"https://github.com/vercel/turborepo/issues/10703"},{"title":"Turbopack: misdetected workspace root (from stray parent lockfile) causes RAM exhaustion (40+ GB) and infinite module-resolution retry on first request · Issue #92978 · vercel/next.js","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/92978"},{"title":"Next.js 16's Turbopack breaks npm link - Steve Harrison","url":"https://steveharrison.dev/next-js-16s-turbopack-breaks-npm-link/"}]

Based on the search results, the error "there must be a path to a root" with Turbopack and pnpm appears to be related to **Turbopack's root path detection issues in pnpm monorepos**. Here's what I found:

## Main Issues

The error occurs when the dev server crashes after reporting that Next.js inferred a workspace root but couldn't find the Next.js package (next/package.json) from the project directory.

The Turbopack Rust binary internally ignores the passed rootPath and performs its own root detection, resolving the App Router directory as the project root instead of the intended project directory.

## Known Causes with pnpm

1. **pnpm Global Virtual Store**: When `enableGlobalVirtualStore: true` is enabled in pnpm v11, node_modules/next is a symlink whose real path points outside the project root, which prevents Turbopack from resolving next/package.json.

2. **Multiple Lock Files**: When moving an app into a monorepo, you should delete the pnpm-lock.yaml file in all project directories, including the main directory and those within apps and packages.

3. **Monorepo Root Detection**: Next.js 16.2.4's Turbopack Rust binary doesn't respect the turbopack.root config option, causing builds to fail when trying to resolve next/package.json.

## Potential Workarounds

A workaround is to set turbopack.root to the common ancestor of the app root and the real path of node_modules/next/package.json, though this can become a very broad directory.

Based on the search results, I found information about this issue:

This is a Turbopack error that occurs with Next.js 15.4.1 and later versions, manifesting as a panic in the module graph code with the message "internal error: entered unreachable code: there must be a path to a root". 

The error originates from `turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/module_graph/mod.rs` and appears to be related to the import tracing operation during compilation.

This issue appears to be reported in the Next.js GitHub discussions (specifically Discussion #82277 on the Vercel/next.js repository), where multiple users have encountered this panic when running `next dev --turbopack`. The error seems to be an internal Turbopack issue that requires investigation and a fix from the Turbopack/Next.js team.

input.prompt: Summarize this discussion about the Turbopack panic "there must be a path to a root" in module_graph/mod.rs: what triggers it, is there a known workaround or fix version, does it relate to pnpm isolated node_modules or symlinks, and what do maintainers say?

result.output:
# Turbopack "Path to Root" Panic Summary

## What Triggers It

The error occurs when **circular dependencies exist in your codebase**. As identified by the original reporter, importing between files creates circular references that Turbopack's module graph resolver cannot handle. The specific example shows `instrumentation.ts` importing from `getEnv.ts`, which then imports back from `instrumentation.ts`.

## Known Workaround

**Resolve circular dependencies in your project.** The maintainer response confirms: "The solution to this issue is to resolve the circular dependency issues in your repo." Newer Next.js versions apparently check for this more aggressively than previous releases.

## Fix Availability

No specific fixed version is mentioned in the discussion. The error appears across versions 15.4.1 through 15.5.3, suggesting it remains an unresolved Turbopack issue at the discussion's conclusion.

## Other Considerations

The discussion does not address pnpm isolated node_modules, symlinks, or specific maintainer statements beyond the circular dependency explanation. Four duplicate discussions were consolidated, indicating this is a recurring problem affecting multiple users.

**Core issue:** This is a **user-side problem** (circular imports) exposed by stricter Turbopack validation, not a Turbopack bug itself.

content.text: The hypothesis chain so far, while we wait: webpack proved the isolated layout is fundamentally buildable (compiled in 4.6 min, only warnings from typeorm's optional drivers + one phantom `playwright` dep at typecheck). Those same 8 typeorm issues are almost certainly what Turbopack chokes on — it panics *while rendering the import trace* of an issue, and typeorm's peer-suffixed `.pnpm` path under isolated breaks the tracer's walk. If externalizing typeorm clears the panic, we have a proper fix rather than a bundler switch.

<task-notification>

Externalization didn't clear it either — so the trigger is deeper (most plausibly the app's real circular imports interacting with the isolated graph; note the same cycles build fine under hoisted, so it's the combination). Three Turbopack-side fixes tried, all failed. The remaining honest path to a **green build on this branch** is the proven one: webpack. Let me flip the build script on this spike branch and drive it to completion:
